response.description_long:
Data platform teams live with a quiet dread: the column renamed in a staging model will silently break the dashboard the CEO opens in the board meeting. Rowfern catches those breaking changes before they reach production, enforcing contracts between data models in the pull request instead of the postmortem. The company was founded in Denver in 2022 by two former data engineers who built the tool after living through that pain themselves.

Rowfern reads a team's dbt project or SQL pipelines and generates a suite of automated checks. It infers schema contracts between producing and consuming models, learns freshness and volume baselines from historical run data, and lets teams write custom assertions in YAML. Every pull request triggers these checks against a sampled snapshot of production data, so a column rename, a join that fans out unexpectedly, or a surge in nulls surfaces immediately. In production, the same checks run on schedule and page the owning team through PagerDuty or Slack when a contract breaks.

The core check engine is open source under an Apache 2.0 license. The commercial platform adds a catalog of contracts, ownership routing, historical baselines, and single sign-on. Cloud plans start at $500 per month per workspace, scaling with the number of models under contract. The open-source engine is free.

Rowfern is used by data platform teams at Chartbeam, Densify Analytics, and Copperline Health, who together run it on more than 4,000 models. The company describes a common adoption pattern: a team experiences a visible data outage — a broken dashboard during a board presentation — and adopts Rowfern to prevent a repeat.

Security-conscious teams can run checks inside their own warehouse over a read-only connection; sampled data never leaves the environment unless the team opts into cloud snapshots. The company holds SOC 2 Type II certification. Rowfern is a 19-person, fully remote team across US time zones.
